<!--
Curated reader manuscript draft.
chapter_id: fast-generation-architectures
generated_baseline_ref: build/reader_edition/chapters/fast-generation-architectures.qmd
live_source_ref: chapters/fast-generation-architectures.qmd@2eb3290f9
This file is a reader-prose derivative only. Preserve claim meaning,
support-state boundaries, source boundaries, proof/test status,
implementation horizons, and release blockers.
-->

# Fast Generation Architectures

Compact generation asked whether a smaller representation had really lowered
total burden. Fast generation asks the same question about time. A faster path
may be useful, but only if accepted output, verifier work, repair, fallback,
memory pressure, and task success still fit inside the route's authority and
evidence boundary.

Fast generation is not the art of making a model talk faster. In this stack,
it is the discipline of deciding when a faster path is still allowed to count
as useful work.

That distinction matters because speed is seductive. A system that produces
twice as many tokens per second can feel smarter even when more of those tokens
need repair, more context has been dropped, more memory pressure has been
created, or the verifier has quietly become the true bottleneck. A serving
system can improve aggregate throughput while doing little for the single
high-risk request that needs a correct artifact. A speculative or diffusion
route can look efficient when proposed spans are counted and much less
impressive after accepted spans, fallback, hardware cost, and task success are
counted.

The stack therefore treats generation speed as a governed route choice. A fast
mode is valuable when it preserves the slow obligations that make output
usable: context adequacy, verifier access, acceptance predicates, fallback,
repair, memory accounting, and evidence recording. Raw velocity is not enough.
Speed becomes progress only after the whole route has paid for acceptance.

The reader promise for Fast Generation is simple: by the end, fast generation
should look less like a leaderboard metric and more like a control plane for
choosing, verifying, and retiring generation modes.

## Problem

The ordinary language-model generation path is serial. A model commits one
token, appends it to the context, runs another step, commits another token, and
keeps going. That left-to-right path is simple to reason about, but it consumes
time that the rest of the stack also needs. Planning, retrieval, tool use,
artifact construction, proof checks, benchmark replay, and human review all
compete for the same latency and compute budget.

If generation is too slow, the system is tempted to spend less time planning or
checking. If generation is accelerated without governance, the system can fail
faster. It may emit fluent output before context has been admitted, before a
claim has been verified, before a tool result has been replayed, or before a
human approval path has had time to matter.

The architecture needs a generation layer that asks a more precise question:
which parts of the output must be generated sequentially, which parts can be
drafted in parallel, which drafts can be accepted cheaply, which uncertain
spans should be repaired, and which tasks require a slower mode because the
risk of accepting bad output is too high?

That is the role of Fast Generation Architectures. It turns generation speed
into a routable, auditable, revocable policy decision.

## Why Existing Approaches Are Insufficient

Standard autoregressive decoding gives the stack a baseline, but it also makes
generation latency a structural tax. Inference-acceleration work attacks that
tax from many directions: speculative decoding, multi-token prediction,
multi-head drafting, feature-level drafting, lookahead branches, early exits,
state-space sequence models, KV-cache systems, diffusion language models, and
hybrid routes.

Those methods are real adjacent work, and the live chapter source matrix links
them as external literature context. But none of that literature, by itself,
answers the stack question. A method can report speedups in its own setting
without proving that this repository has reproduced them, that a governed
planner can select them safely, that the verifier can keep up, or that the
accepted artifact is better per second after repair and fallback are counted.

The insufficient approach is to count what was easy to measure and then let the
result stand for intelligence. Proposed tokens are not accepted tokens.
Accepted text is not yet a trusted artifact. Aggregate throughput is not the
same thing as single-request adequacy. A lower-latency route is not equivalent
to a safer or more capable route unless the evidence packet says what moved,
what failed, what was repaired, and what was paid elsewhere.

The accounting unit has to be stricter than tokens per second. The stack cares
about accepted or verified correct output per wall-clock time, and about task
success per wall-clock time, under a declared task and risk model. Even those
metrics are not enough alone. They become meaningful only when the route also
records verification cost, memory pressure, fallback, repair, and promotion
decision.

This is why fast generation cannot live as a loose collection of tricks. It
needs a mode registry, a selector, an acceptance loop, and a no-promotion rule
for latency-only wins.

## Core Claim

Fast generation should be treated as a governed, planner-selected choice among
autoregressive, speculative, multi-token, diffusion, early-exit, state-space,
KV-cache, and hybrid modes selected by task risk, quality target, latency
budget, compute, memory, and verification burden.

The evidence boundary is architectural argument. The current repository
contains a generation-mode record fixture and finite Lean predicates for
promotion discipline. It also contains source-noted external literature for
the method taxonomy and a local public-safe task-bundle accounting replay,
`fast_generation_task_bundle_2026_07_02_local`. That replay selects
`route://fast-template-verified` over an autoregressive reference under
deterministic cost units and rejects `route://latency-only-proxy`, but it is a
no model-speed or deployment claim. The accepted no-promotion decision at
`evidence_transitions/v1_x_measured/fast_generation_task_bundle_no_change.json`
keeps that slice from becoming a speed, model-quality, serving-throughput,
route-selector, benchmark, deployed-routing, or chapter-core support claim. It
does not contain a local decoding benchmark, diffusion benchmark, MTP run,
speculative-acceptance experiment, serving deployment, KV-cache audit, model
useful-solution-per-second result, or reproduced speedup.

The claim is not that one acceleration family wins. The claim is that the
stack needs a policy surface where those families can be compared by accepted
work, verifier cost, risk class, memory burden, fallback, and task success.

## Mechanism

Fast generation is the time-domain companion to compact generation. In
compression, the stack asks whether a smaller representation can reconstruct or
serve the needed artifact without hiding residuals. In fast generation, it asks
whether a faster route can produce accepted useful work without hiding rejected
drafts, verifier load, repair cost, memory pressure, or risk.

The generation-mode record is therefore a receipt for time. It records what
the route tried to save, which mode was selected, what the generator proposed,
what the verifier accepted, what was rejected or repaired, what fallback ran,
and whether the useful solution rate improved after the whole route was
counted.

The operational boundary begins with a mode selector.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A["Goal and risk tier"] --> B["PlanForge mode selector"]
  C["VCM context packet"] --> B
  D["Latency, compute, memory budget"] --> B
  B -- "select by task, risk, and budget" --> E{"Generation mode"}
  E -- "known baseline" --> F["Autoregressive route"]
  E -- "draft route" --> G["Speculative or MTP route"]
  E -- "sketch route" --> H["Diffusion or sketch-first route"]
  E -- "serving route" --> I["Early-exit, state-space, or KV-cache route"]
  E -- "composed route" --> J["Hybrid route"]
  F --> K["Proposed tokens, spans, or artifact fragments"]
  G --> K
  H --> K
  I --> K
  J --> K
  K --> L["Verifier and acceptance predicate"]
  L -- "accepted" --> M["Accepted verified output"]
  L -- "rejected or partial" --> N["Repair, fallback, or slower mode"]
  M --> O["Talos artifact and evidence record"]
  N --> P["Rejected work, residual, and verifier cost"]
  O --> Q["Speed-quality ledger"]
  P --> Q
  Q --> R["SCF promotion, quarantine, or retirement"]
```

The selector belongs near planning because the right generation mode depends
on task shape. A low-risk autocomplete can tolerate a cheap accelerated path.
A code patch can use a draft route if tests and review are strong enough. A
high-risk claim, authority escalation, or irreversible action can force a
slower verified route even when a faster route exists.

The context packet matters because speed cannot compensate for the wrong
context. A route that drafts quickly from stale, tainted, under-admitted, or
over-compressed context should fail at the context or acceptance boundary, not
receive credit for low latency. VCM supplies the bounded context packet;
Spinoza-style verification and task-specific validators decide what can be
accepted; Talos turns accepted output into artifacts; Benchmaxxing records the
speed-quality evidence; SCF decides whether the mode is default,
experimental, quarantined, or retired.

The mechanism is deliberately conservative. Proposed work is logged, but only
accepted work counts. Rejected spans are not hidden. Fallback is not treated as
an implementation detail. Memory pressure and verifier cost are part of the
route, not footnotes.

## Generation-Mode Families

The taxonomy is plural because generation speed is not one bottleneck.

The autoregressive baseline remains the fallback and measurement anchor. It is
slow, but it gives the stack a known left-to-right route against which faster
routes can be compared.

Multi-token prediction, speculative decoding, multi-head internal drafting,
and feature-level drafting all try to propose more future work from a cheaper
path. Their central risk is the difference between proposed and accepted work.
A draft route that proposes many tokens but accepts few may shift the cost to
verification, repair, or fallback.

Lookahead and trie-style retrieval reuse likely continuations from prior
patterns. They are attractive when work repeats, but the stack has to guard
against stale procedural shortcuts. A retrieved branch must still be checked
against the current task, source state, and risk class.

Diffusion language modeling and sketch-first generation attack seriality by
refining many positions or spans in parallel. That changes the shape of the
verification burden. The stack must verify span commitments, uncertainty
repair, and final task success, not just report that the generation process was
less left-to-right.

Early-exit and self-speculative inference use cheaper intermediate outputs as
drafts and later layers as verifiers or correctors. State-space and recurrent
alternatives attack sequence-processing cost through a different substrate.
KV-cache and serving-layer accelerators improve memory layout, batching, and
throughput around generation. These are useful axes, but they do not collapse
into one claim. A serving-memory win, a decoding win, a training-objective win,
and a task-success win need different records.

Hybrid routes are especially tempting. A future system might combine
autoregressive anchors, multi-token drafting, diffusion repair, retrieval
branches, and verifier-guided selection. The book can name that endpoint as an
architecture target, but it cannot treat it as implemented. Hybrid routes stay
experimental until their records show accepted output, task success, verifier
cost, memory pressure, fallback, and negative cases.

## Interfaces

The generation layer speaks through a Generation Mode Record.

Minimum fields include:

- `task_id`
- `risk_tier`
- `latency_budget`
- `compute_budget`
- `memory_budget`
- `context_packet_id`
- `generation_mode`
- `draft_source`
- `verifier`
- `acceptance_predicate`
- `proposed_tokens_or_spans`
- `accepted_tokens_or_spans`
- `wall_clock_time`
- `quality_or_pass_result`
- `repair_or_fallback`
- `promotion_decision`
- `non_claims`

Those fields look mundane because the record is meant to resist laundering. A
fast-generation chapter should not become a pile of impressive method names
without a route, verifier, fallback, or promotion rule.

PlanForge chooses the generation mode. VCM supplies the bounded context.
Generators emit drafts, sketches, spans, or candidates. Spinoza-style
verifiers, test harnesses, or task-specific validators check acceptance
predicates. Talos turns accepted output into artifacts. Benchmark ratchets
compare speed and quality. Stable capability fields govern whether the mode is
default, experimental, quarantined, or retired.

The consumer policy is as important as the method list. A high-risk task can
disallow a fast route. A low-risk draft can accept provisional output with an
explicit fallback. A budget-constrained task can reduce scope rather than
quietly lowering the verification bar.

## Invariants

The core invariant is that raw speed cannot promote a generation mode by
itself.

- Every accelerated route names its mode, verifier, acceptance predicate, risk
  tier, and fallback.
- Proposed output is counted separately from accepted output.
- Accepted output is counted separately from useful task success.
- High-risk tasks can force slower verified generation.
- Verification cost, memory pressure, repair, and fallback are part of the
  route cost.
- External method reports remain source-reported until reproduced, fully
  inspected, or promoted through a separate accepted evidence transition.
- A failed or missing verifier blocks promotion, even if latency improves.

These invariants do not forbid fast decoding. They prevent fast decoding from
being mistaken for demonstrated capability. They keep acceleration tied to the
same evidence discipline as the rest of the stack.

## Failure Modes

Fast generation fails when speed is measured at the wrong boundary.

The first failure mode is quality hiding. A faster route emits more output, but
more of it is wrong, partial, under-sourced, or expensive to repair. The route
looks good only while repair and review are off the ledger.

The second is verifier inversion. The generator becomes faster, but the
verifier becomes the bottleneck. If verifier capacity is scarce, the system can
produce a backlog of plausible drafts that cannot be accepted in time.

The third is distribution drift. Draft heads, latent predictors, retrieval
branches, diffusion sketches, or cached continuations can leave the region
where the acceptance predicate was reliable. A mode that works for ordinary
text may fail for code, proofs, citations, tool plans, or authority-bearing
actions.

The fourth is memory illusion. KV-cache management, batching, and serving
optimizations improve throughput, but the stack mistakes aggregate serving
capacity for single-request quality or lower risk.

The fifth is multi-seed waste. Running many trajectories can improve
robustness only if selection and verification are cheap enough to beat a
single stronger route. Otherwise, multi-seed generation is more expensive
candidate production with a nicer story.

The sixth is fallback omission. The route accepts uncertainty without a
slower path, repair path, residual record, or quarantine. That is the failure
Fast Generation is most designed to prevent.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

The minimum viable implementation is not a universal fast decoder. It is a
generation-mode registry and benchmark record.

The first useful slice should compare a plain baseline with one
verifier-preserving accelerated path on a small task suite. The repository now
has that slice in bounded form. The task-bundle replay compares an
autoregressive reference, `route://fast-template-verified`, and
`route://latency-only-proxy` over four public-safe receipt tasks. The verified
route passes 4/4 tasks at 264 deterministic cost units versus 632 for the
baseline. The latency-only route is cheaper at 176 cost units, but it is
rejected because it fails verifier, fallback, residual, and support-state
boundaries.

That result is deliberately small. It is an accounting replay, not a decoding
benchmark. It shows how the book will record baseline, verifier, fallback,
residual, negative-control, and non-claim boundaries before any future fast
generation result is allowed to matter.

There is also a narrower Project Theseus generation-mode import. The imported
public-safe summary records 18 modes, 13 comparisons, zero hard gaps, zero
promotable comparisons, and five accepted-span speed-lift warnings with zero
useful-solution-per-second evidence. The book-side validators and finite Lean
fixture preserve that as negative promotion evidence: raw speed warnings do not
become a speedup claim when task passes and useful-solution evidence are absent.

The related Theseus public task-bundle import gives Fast Generation one richer
implementation-reference boundary. `theseus_public_task_bundle_import_2026_07_03_local`
records 64 public BigCodeBench metadata-only tasks, 0 public training rows, 0
task-level regressions, 18 benchmark gates, and visible residual/artifact-gap
fields. That helps the Fast Generation argument talk about quality and residual accounting
beside speed, but the clean live Theseus replay remains unclaimed and the
import does not prove model quality, benchmark superiority, generation speed,
useful-solution-per-second improvement, or a support-state transition.

Boundary sentence: 0 task-level regressions; clean live Theseus replay remains
unclaimed.

That is still useful. A record-shape fixture gives the stack a place to put
future evidence without letting future evidence blur the boundaries. It can
record a negative result honestly: a route was faster, but useful verified
work per second went down after verifier cost, repair, memory pressure, and
fallback were counted.

## Beyond the State of the Art

The mature endpoint is a governed generation-mode control plane.

Such a controller would let the stack choose among slow, fast, speculative,
parallel, recurrent, cached, and hybrid generation routes without allowing raw
throughput to masquerade as capability. It would maintain a registry of modes,
a task-risk classifier, context-admission checks, draft-source records,
verifier receipts, acceptance predicates, repair and fallback routes,
memory-pressure accounting, and promotion gates.

In that mature system, fast generation is not a single model trick. It is a
portfolio of routeable specialists. A speculative route might be default for
low-risk drafting. A multi-token route might be allowed for boilerplate but
blocked for evidence-bearing claims. A diffusion or hybrid route might propose
sketches that a slower verifier repairs. A serving-memory route might improve
throughput without making any claim about reasoning quality. A high-risk route
can still demand the slow baseline if that is what adequate verification
requires.

The evidence surface would separate proposed spans from accepted spans,
wall-clock latency from useful solution rate, raw throughput from verifier
cost, and serving-layer wins from quality claims. Accepted acceleration paths
would become routable specialists. Rejected drafts would become evidence about
verifier bottlenecks. Drift would become readiness residual. High-risk tasks
would keep their right to be slow.

That endpoint remains proposed architecture. Support stays at `argument` until
mode-selection records, latency/quality comparisons, verifier acceptance
traces, raw-speed failure cases, fallback routes, and promotion packets show
where fast generation is adequate and where it is only fast.

## Summary

Fast Generation Architectures defines the stack's generation substrate. Its
thesis is not "make tokens faster." Its thesis is that generation speed becomes
useful only when the stack can select a mode, verify accepted output, count
repair and fallback, account for memory and verifier burden, and promote the
mode through evidence rather than excitement.

The chapter connects compression, routing, execution, evidence, and external
inference-acceleration literature. PlanForge selects the route. VCM supplies
context. The generator drafts. Verifiers accept or reject. Talos artifactizes.
Benchmark ratchets measure. Stable capability fields decide whether the mode
is ready, experimental, quarantined, or retired.

The conservative boundary is the point: speed becomes an architecture win only
when the accepted-output ledger survives contact with verification bandwidth,
memory pressure, fallback cost, and downstream task success.

## Handoff

Fast generation reduces time pressure at the output boundary, but stored and
routed artifacts still need compression contracts of their own. RankFold,
NeuralFold, and Artifact Compression narrows the focus to reusable artifacts:
how archive forms, deterministic decode checks, probes, fallback, utility, and
cost accounting decide whether a compressed artifact can safely accompany or
stand in for its fuller source. Speed is a route decision over time; artifact
compression is the same discipline applied to things the stack wants to keep.
